questions:
You are a new Mac user in Singapore who has just finished reviewing Apple’s official guidance on getting started, switching from Windows, and protecting your privacy. Now you want to (1) review the official purchasing and return conditions that apply when buying directly from Apple within Singapore, and (2) explore the comprehensive navigation page that lists all Apple product categories, services, and support resources.

Using Apple’s official Singapore online store terms and its global site-wide navigation directory, explain the following: What is the standard return window for most products, what special conditions apply to volume purchases and iPhone returns, and what refund restrictions are specific to Singapore? Additionally, from the global navigation directory, identify the main Mac product lineup currently listed and at least three support or service options available to Mac users.

Provide a structured explanation covering both sources.

Within the official guidance for Apple’s desktop operating system, several core introductory sections are designed to help users begin using their computer, transition from another platform, integrate with other devices in the same ecosystem, explore the latest major operating system release, manage privacy and security (including credential recovery), understand model-specific essentials, and learn about the platform’s built-in artificial intelligence system. Identify each of these distinct sections and explain the primary purpose or capabilities highlighted in each one. Your response should clearly distinguish how each section supports a different aspect of onboarding, ecosystem connectivity, new feature discovery, data protection, device familiarization, or AI integration.

A suite of on-device artificial intelligence features was rolled out across a major technology company’s 2025–2026 operating system releases for its smartphones, tablets, computers, mixed-reality headset, and wearable devices. Identify every hardware category that officially supports this AI system. For each category, specify: (1) the minimum supported device models or chip generation, (2) the minimum operating system version required at launch, (3) any stated storage or pairing prerequisites, and (4) at least one feature that is either exclusive to that category or explicitly limited to it (for example, features available only on the wearable, only on the headset, or only on the smartphone). Conclude by summarizing any major regional or language-based restrictions that affect availability.

rounds.question:
You are a new Mac user in Singapore who has just finished reviewing Apple’s official guidance on getting started, switching from Windows, and protecting your privacy. Now you want to (1) review the official purchasing and return conditions that apply when buying directly from Apple within Singapore, and (2) explore the comprehensive navigation page that lists all Apple product categories, services, and support resources.

Using Apple’s official Singapore online store terms and its global site-wide navigation directory, explain the following: What is the standard return window for most products, what special conditions apply to volume purchases and iPhone returns, and what refund restrictions are specific to Singapore? Additionally, from the global navigation directory, identify the main Mac product lineup currently listed and at least three support or service options available to Mac users.

Provide a structured explanation covering both sources.
checklist_width: 7-day return window + 25% restocking fee for volume purchases
iPhone return condition about wireless service agreement and unauthorized modification (jailbreaking) warning
Refunds remitted only to a Singapore bank account matching payor name and not outside Singapore
Mac product lineup: MacBook Air, MacBook Pro, iMac, Mac mini, Mac Studio, Mac Pro
At least three Mac support/service options: AppleCare, Genius Bar, Mac Support, Manuals, Apple Discussions, Apple Store Pickup, Personal Setup
checklist_depth: Singapore Consumer Store (Apple Online Store Singapore terms and conditions) + logic: official purchasing and return conditions for buyers within Singapore
Apple Site Map + logic: comprehensive global navigation directory listing product categories, services, and support resources including Mac lineup
rounds.rationale: The question uses the context of a new Mac user (from the Mac User Guide domain) as a logical filter to justify consulting official purchasing terms and the global navigation directory. It masks the specific document names by describing them functionally (Singapore purchasing terms and global navigation directory). The task requires aggregating dispersed policy details (returns, refunds, special conditions) and separately compiling product and support listings from the navigation directory, ensuring both deep identification and wide information synthesis.
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Singapore Consumer Store Thanks for shopping at Apple. We appreciate the fact that you like to buy the cool stuff we build. We also want to make sure you have a rewarding expe
rounds.answer_a:
When purchasing directly from Apple in Singapore, the standard return window for most products is 14 calendar days from the date you receive them. However, special conditions apply to certain purchases. For volume purchases, if you have ordered more than two units of the same product, you must return all units of that product at the same time. A significant condition applies to iPhones: they are not eligible for a return once the product box has been opened. For refunds specific to Singapore, if you paid by cash-on-delivery or bank transfer, Apple requires you to provide your bank details and will issue the refund via a direct bank transfer [1].

Exploring Apple's global navigation directory reveals the comprehensive product and support ecosystem. The main Mac product lineup currently listed includes the MacBook Air, MacBook Pro, iMac, Mac mini, Mac Studio, and Mac Pro. For support and services, Mac users have several options available through this directory. Key resources include scheduling a "Genius Bar Reservation" for hands-on technical support at an Apple Store, purchasing "AppleCare+" to extend warranty coverage and technical support, and utilizing the "Apple Trade In" program to get credit for an old device towards a new purchase [2].

## References
[1] Apple. (2024). *Sales and Refunds - Apple Store (Singapore)*. Retrieved from apple.com/sg/shop/browse/open/salespolicies.
[2] Apple. (2024). *Site Map*. Retrieved from apple.com/sitemap/.
rounds.answer_b:
Apple's official Singapore online store operates under specific purchasing and return policies, while the global Apple site directory provides a comprehensive overview of all products and services.

## Return Window and Conditions

The standard return window for most products purchased directly from Apple Singapore is **14 calendar days** from the date of delivery [1]. Products must be returned in their original condition with packaging and accessories. However, several special conditions apply:

**Volume Purchases:** Orders exceeding a specified quantity threshold (typically 10 or more of the same product) are not eligible for the standard return policy under Apple's consumer terms and may require engagement with Apple's business sales team [1].

**iPhone Returns:** iPhones must be returned within the same 14-day window, but customers must **deactivate** any carrier activation and remove the device from their Apple ID and Find My iPhone before returning. Opened iPhones may also be subject to a restocking fee [1].

## Singapore-Specific Refund Restrictions

Refunds in Singapore are processed back to the original payment method. Importantly, refunds for purchases made via certain local payment methods may take additional processing time. Products purchased with promotional gift cards may have the gift card value deducted from the refund amount. Singapore consumer law under the Lemon Law provides additional statutory protections, but Apple's voluntary return policy exists alongside—not in replacement of—these rights [1][2].

## Mac Product Lineup (Global Navigation Directory)

The Apple site map lists the following Mac products: **MacBook Air, MacBook Pro, iMac, Mac mini, Mac Studio, Mac Pro,** and related **displays** [3].

## Support and Service Options for Mac Users

From the global navigation directory, at least three support/service resources include: **(1) Mac Support pages, (2) AppleCare+ coverage plans, (3) Genius Bar appointments** at Apple Stores, and additionally the **Apple Community forums** [3].

## References
[1] Apple Singapore Online Store – Sales & Refund Policy: https://www.apple.com/sg/shop/help/returns_refund
[2] Consumers Association of Singapore – Lemon Law: https://www.case.org.sg
[3] Apple Site Map: https://www.apple.com/sitemap/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified the relevant sources—Apple Singapore Online Store terms for purchasing/returns and Apple’s global sitemap for product lineup and support resources. So both pass the core entity check.

Width/Completeness: Both fail key checklist details. Neither mentions the required **7-day return window with 25% restocking fee for volume purchases**. Both provide incorrect or unsupported conditions for iPhone returns (missing the required reference to wireless service agreement obligations and unauthorized modification/jailbreaking warning). Critically, neither clearly states the Singapore-specific refund restriction that refunds are remitted only to a Singapore bank account matching the payor name and not outside Singapore. These are major factual omissions, so both fail WIDE aggregation.

Mac lineup: Both correctly list the full Mac lineup (MacBook Air, MacBook Pro, iMac, Mac mini, Mac Studio, Mac Pro). Both provide at least three support/service options, though Agent B’s are more directly aligned with support resources (Mac Support, AppleCare+, Genius Bar, Community forums), while Agent A includes Apple Trade In, which is less clearly a Mac support resource.

Presentation & UX: Agent B provides clearer structure, stronger formatting, and slightly better scannability. However, because both contain significant factual inaccuracies on required return and refund conditions, neither qualifies as high quality.

Therefore, this is a LOW-QUALITY TIE: both pass entity identification but fail multiple mandatory checklist details.

rounds.question: Within the official guidance for Apple’s desktop operating system, several core introductory sections are designed to help users begin using their computer, transition from another platform, integrate with other devices in the same ecosystem, explore the latest major operating system release, manage privacy and security (including credential recovery), understand model-specific essentials, and learn about the platform’s built-in artificial intelligence system. Identify each of these distinct sections and explain the primary purpose or capabilities highlighted in each one. Your response should clearly distinguish how each section supports a different aspect of onboarding, ecosystem connectivity, new feature discovery, data protection, device familiarization, or AI integration.
checklist_width: Describes initial setup guidance including wallpaper selection, changing common settings, and starting apps
Explains transition guidance for users switching from Windows, including navigation help and file transfer from PC
Details cross-device integration features such as viewing/controlling iPhone, copy-paste between devices
Summarizes features of the latest OS release including new design, phone calls from Mac, enhanced search actions, and automatic translations
Outlines privacy and security tools including password management and private browsing
Mentions recovery of recently deleted passwords or passkeys
References model-specific getting started essentials for new Mac owners
Explains the built-in AI system as personal, private, and integrated into the operating system
checklist_depth: Get Started with Your Mac + Identified as the introductory setup section
Switched from Windows to Mac + Identified as the transition guide for former PC users
Use Your Mac with Other Apple Devices + Identified as the ecosystem integration section
What’s New in the Latest macOS Release (macOS Tahoe) + Identified as the new feature overview section
Control Your Privacy and Security + Identified as the data protection section
Recover a Recently Deleted Password + Identified as the credential recovery guidance
Get to Know Your Mac + Identified as the model-specific essentials guide
Apple Intelligence + Identified as the built-in AI system integrated into macOS
rounds.rationale: The question uses categorical logic (onboarding, switching, ecosystem integration, OS updates, security, recovery, device basics, and AI) to mask the exact section titles (Deep reasoning). To answer fully, a respondent must aggregate distinct details about each separate section’s purpose and capabilities rather than rely on a single source summary (Wide coverage).
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Get started with your Mac You can set up and start using your new Mac in no time. Choose a wallpaper for your desktop, change common settings, start using apps and more. Learn
rounds.answer_a:
Within Apple's official desktop operating system guidance, distinct sections address specific user needs from onboarding to advanced feature integration.

*   **Intro to your Mac:** This foundational section serves as the primary onboarding tool, explaining core navigation elements like the desktop, Dock, Menu Bar, and Finder to help new users begin using their computer [1].
*   **Switch from Windows to Mac:** To facilitate platform transition, this guide focuses on the Migration Assistant tool, which transfers user accounts, documents, and settings from a Windows PC [2].
*   **Use your Mac with iCloud and Continuity:** This section highlights ecosystem connectivity, detailing features like Handoff, Universal Clipboard, and Sidecar that allow seamless workflow between a Mac, iPhone, and iPad [3].
*   **What’s new in macOS:** For feature discovery, this area showcases the latest capabilities and enhancements introduced in the most recent major OS release, such as new widgets or app updates [4].
*   **Security and privacy on Mac:** Focused on data protection, this section explains how to manage app permissions, use built-in encryption, and provides critical steps for credential recovery if you forget your login or Apple ID password [5].
*   **Mac Essentials:** For device familiarization, model-specific guides (e.g., "MacBook Air Essentials") detail the hardware ports, unique functions, and included accessories for that particular computer [6].
*   **Apple Intelligence:** This section introduces the platform's integrated artificial intelligence, explaining its capabilities for summarizing text, creating images, and enhancing Siri to provide a more personalized and productive user experience [7].

## References
[1] Apple Support. (n.d.). *Intro to the Mac desktop*. Retrieved from support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/mchlp1058/mac.
[2] Apple Support. (n.d.). *Move your data from a Windows PC to a Mac*. Retrieved from support.apple.com/en-us/HT204087.
[3] Apple Support. (n.d.). *Use Continuity to work across Apple devices*. Retrieved from support.apple.com/en-us/HT204681.
[4] Apple. (n.d.). *macOS Sonoma is here*. Retrieved from apple.com/macos/sonoma.
[5] Apple Support. (n.d.). *Security and privacy on Mac*. Retrieved from support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/security-privacy-on-mac-mchlp27201/mac.
[6] Apple Support. (n.d.). *MacBook Air Essentials*. Retrieved from support.apple.com/guide/macbook-air/welcome/mac.
[7] Apple. (n.d.). *Apple Intelligence*. Retrieved from apple.com/apple-intelligence/.
rounds.answer_b:
The official macOS User Guide from Apple includes several core introductory sections designed to onboard users and help them get the most from their Mac. Here are the distinct sections and their purposes:

1. **Get Started**: This section covers the fundamentals of setting up and beginning to use your Mac, including initial configuration, desktop navigation, and basic system operations [1].

2. **Switch from PC to Mac**: Targeted at Windows users transitioning to macOS, this section explains how to migrate files, understand interface differences, and adapt workflows from a PC environment [1].

3. **Use Mac with Other Apple Devices (Continuity)**: This section highlights ecosystem integration features like Handoff, AirDrop, Universal Clipboard, Sidecar, and iPhone Mirroring, enabling seamless connectivity between Mac, iPhone, iPad, and Apple Watch [1][2].

4. **What's New in macOS Sequoia**: This section showcases the latest features introduced in the current major release, helping users discover new capabilities such as window tiling, iPhone Mirroring enhancements, and Safari improvements [1][2].

5. **Privacy and Security / If You Forget Your Apple ID Password**: These sections address data protection, privacy controls, and credential recovery. Users learn about managing app permissions, FileVault encryption, and steps to reset their Apple ID password if locked out [1].

6. **Mac Essentials (model-specific)**: Tailored to specific Mac models (MacBook Air, MacBook Pro, iMac, etc.), these guides cover hardware-specific features, ports, and unique capabilities relevant to each device [1].

7. **Apple Intelligence**: This section introduces Apple's built-in AI system, highlighting features like Writing Tools, Image Playground, Genmoji, enhanced Siri capabilities, and intelligent summarization powered by on-device and cloud-based machine learning [1][2].

## References
[1] Apple. "macOS Sequoia User Guide." https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/welcome/mac
[2] Apple. "What's new in macOS Sequoia." https://support.apple.com/en-us/111901
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identified the core entities (Deep Logic pass): Get Started, Switch from Windows, Use with Other Apple Devices, What’s New in macOS, Privacy & Security, Password Recovery, Model-Specific Essentials, and Apple Intelligence. 

On Width/Completeness, both covered most checklist elements but missed a specific detail about recovering recently deleted passwords/passkeys. Agent A referenced credential recovery generally, while Agent B focused on Apple ID reset—neither explicitly mentioned deleted passkeys. Both described ecosystem integration and AI integration well. However, Agent B provided more concrete examples of new OS features (e.g., window tiling, iPhone Mirroring enhancements) and referenced the current release (Sequoia), whereas Agent A cited Sonoma, which is less current. 

In terms of User Experience and Presentation, both used clear structure and bullets. Agent A had more citations, but Agent B’s numbered format and clearer mapping between section title and purpose improved scannability and alignment with the query’s onboarding/ecosystem/privacy framing. 

Since both are largely accurate and comprehensive, and Agent B edges ahead on specificity and clarity without introducing errors, the verdict is B_BETTER.

lines: -> WINNER is B. Targeting Loser Failure: NONE.

rounds.question: A suite of on-device artificial intelligence features was rolled out across a major technology company’s 2025–2026 operating system releases for its smartphones, tablets, computers, mixed-reality headset, and wearable devices. Identify every hardware category that officially supports this AI system. For each category, specify: (1) the minimum supported device models or chip generation, (2) the minimum operating system version required at launch, (3) any stated storage or pairing prerequisites, and (4) at least one feature that is either exclusive to that category or explicitly limited to it (for example, features available only on the wearable, only on the headset, or only on the smartphone). Conclude by summarizing any major regional or language-based restrictions that affect availability.
checklist_width: Smartphone: minimum supported models (e.g., specific Pro or later series), minimum OS version, storage requirement (7 GB), example exclusive/limited feature (e.g., Visual intelligence or Live Translation with AirPods), regional restrictions (e.g., China mainland limitations, EU limitation for AirPods translation)
Tablet: minimum supported models or chip generation (e.g., A17 Pro or M1 and later), minimum OS version, storage requirement, example feature supported on tablet (e.g., Image Wand or Writing Tools)
Computer: minimum supported chip generation (M1 or later), minimum OS version, storage requirement, example feature (e.g., systemwide Writing Tools or ChatGPT integration)
Mixed-reality headset: supported device name, minimum visionOS version, language availability limitations, example supported feature (e.g., Genmoji or Image Playground)
Wearable: minimum supported watch models, minimum watchOS version, pairing requirement with compatible smartphone, example limited feature (e.g., Workout Buddy or Live Translation in Messages)
checklist_depth: Smartphone category correctly identified + proof via compatible Pro/later models and required iOS version
Tablet category correctly identified + proof via chip requirement (A17 Pro or M1+) and required iPadOS version
Computer category correctly identified + proof via Apple silicon (M1+) and required macOS version
Mixed-reality headset correctly identified + proof via required visionOS version
Wearable correctly identified + proof via specific Series/Ultra/SE models and pairing logic with AI-enabled smartphone
rounds.rationale: The question uses deep reasoning by referring indirectly to a cross-platform AI system introduced in specific OS generations, requiring the agent to infer the exact product categories from compatibility logic rather than being told their names. It enforces wide aggregation by demanding model thresholds, OS versions, storage/pairing conditions, exclusive features, and regional restrictions across all supported device types, ensuring information must be compiled from multiple official product and support sources.
source_nodes: How to get Apple Intelligence - Apple Support
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Apple Site Map About Apple Apple Leadership Career Opportunities Investors Contact Apple Legal Choose Your Country or Region Product Feedback Apple Values Environment Supply C
rounds.answer_a:
Apple Intelligence, an on-device AI system, is officially supported on select iPhone, iPad, and Mac hardware. The system will not be available on Apple Watch or Apple Vision Pro at launch [1, 2].

**iPhone**
*   **Minimum Models:** iPhone 15 Pro and iPhone 15 Pro Max, requiring the A17 Pro chip or newer [3].
*   **Minimum OS:** iOS 18 [1].
*   **Prerequisites:** None stated beyond having a compatible device and OS.
*   **Limited Feature:** The "Reduce Interruptions" focus mode uses on-device intelligence to analyze notifications and surface only the most urgent ones, a feature uniquely tailored to the smartphone’s role as a constant communication device [1].

**iPad**
*   **Minimum Models:** iPad Air and iPad Pro models equipped with an M1 chip or later [3].
*   **Minimum OS:** iPadOS 18 [1].
*   **Prerequisites:** None stated.
*   **Limited Feature:** The Image Wand tool works with Apple Pencil in the Notes app, allowing users to circle a rough sketch to have a polished image generated in its place. This capability is explicitly linked to the iPad's unique input hardware [4].

**Mac**
*   **Minimum Models:** All Mac models with an M1 chip or later, including MacBook Air, MacBook Pro, iMac, and Mac mini [3].
*   **Minimum OS:** macOS Sequoia [1].
*   **Prerequisites:** None stated.
*   **Limited Feature:** Enhanced Siri offers screen awareness, enabling it to understand on-screen content and execute complex, multi-step actions across different applications. This function is particularly powerful within the Mac's multitasking desktop environment [2].

Upon its release in the fall of 2024, Apple Intelligence will launch in beta and will be available only in U.S. English. Apple has confirmed that features and additional language support will be rolled out over the following year [1, 3].

## References
[1] Apple. (2024, June 10). *Apple’s breakthrough personal intelligence system, Apple Intelligence, coming to iPhone, iPad, and Mac*. Apple Newsroom. Retrieved from apple.com/newsroom/2024/06/introducing-apple-intelligence-for-iphone-ipad-and-mac/.
[2] The Verge. (2024, June 10). *Apple Intelligence: every new AI feature coming to the iPhone and Mac*. Retrieved from theverge.com/2024/6/10/24175402/apple-intelligence-ai-ios-18-macos-sequoia-siri-features.
[3] MacRumors. (2024, June 14). *Apple Intelligence: Compatible Devices, Release Date, and More*. Retrieved from macrumors.com/guide/apple-intelligence/.
[4] Apple. (2024). *iPadOS 18 Preview*. Apple. Retrieved from apple.com/ipados/ipados-18-preview/.
rounds.answer_b:
Apple Intelligence is supported across five hardware categories in Apple's 2025–2026 OS releases:

**1. iPhone (Smartphone)**
- **Minimum models:** iPhone 15 Pro, iPhone 15 Pro Max, or any iPhone 16 model (requires A17 Pro chip or later) [1][2].
- **Minimum OS:** iOS 18.1 at launch [1].
- **Prerequisites:** Siri and device language set to a supported language; at least 4GB RAM implied by chip requirement [2].
- **Exclusive feature:** Visual Intelligence using the Camera Control button (iPhone 16 series only) for real-time object and place identification [3].

**2. iPad (Tablet)**
- **Minimum models:** iPad Pro (M1 or later), iPad Air (M1 or later), iPad mini (A17 Pro) [1][2].
- **Minimum OS:** iPadOS 18.1 [1].
- **Prerequisites:** Same language/region requirements; sufficient storage for on-device models.
- **Exclusive/highlighted feature:** Handwriting refinement with Smart Script in Notes, leveraging Apple Pencil [3].

**3. Mac (Computer)**
- **Minimum chip:** Apple silicon (M1 or later) [1][2].
- **Minimum OS:** macOS 15.1 (Sequoia) [1].
- **Prerequisites:** 16GB unified memory recommended for expanded local model processing; supported language configured [2].
- **Exclusive feature:** Priority Notifications and Writing Tools integrated system-wide across desktop productivity apps with broader contextual window support [3].

**4. Apple Vision Pro (Mixed-Reality Headset)**
- **Minimum model:** Apple Vision Pro (M2 chip) [4].
- **Minimum OS:** visionOS 2.1 [4].
- **Prerequisites:** Apple Account signed in with supported language/region.
- **Exclusive feature:** Spatial AI-driven Writing Tools and intelligent summarization within the immersive spatial computing environment [4].

**5. Apple Watch (Wearable)**
- **Minimum models:** Apple Watch Series 10, Apple Watch Ultra 2 [5].
- **Minimum OS:** watchOS 11.1 [5].
- **Prerequisites:** Must be paired with a compatible Apple Intelligence-supported iPhone [5].
- **Exclusive feature:** Summarized notifications and Smart Reply on-wrist, processed via the paired iPhone [5].

**Regional/Language Restrictions:** Apple Intelligence launched initially in U.S. English only (December 2024 added localized English for UK, Australia, Canada, and others). Additional languages—Chinese, French, German, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Portuguese, Spanish, Vietnamese—are rolling out through 2025. EU availability for iPhone was delayed due to Digital Markets Act compliance, arriving with iOS 18.4 in April 2025 [1][3].

## References
[1] Apple. "Apple Intelligence – Supported devices and requirements." https://support.apple.com/en-us/111907
[2] Apple. "iOS 18 – Apple Intelligence." https://www.apple.com/apple-intelligence/
[3] Apple Newsroom. "Apple Intelligence is available starting with iOS 18.1." https://www.apple.com/newsroom/2024/10/apple-intelligence-is-available-today-on-iphone-ipad-and-mac/
[4] Apple. "visionOS 2 updates." https://support.apple.com/en-us/111900
[5] Apple. "watchOS 11 – Apple Intelligence features." https://support.apple.com/guide/watch/apple-intelligence-apd1499b5c43/watchos
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Agent A incorrectly states that Apple Intelligence will not be available on Apple Watch or Apple Vision Pro, directly contradicting the ground truth requirement that five hardware categories (smartphone, tablet, computer, mixed-reality headset, wearable) support the system. This is a core entity failure. Agent B correctly identifies all five categories and provides model, chip, and OS requirements for each.

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent A only covers iPhone, iPad, and Mac, omitting headset and wearable entirely, and fails to mention storage requirements (e.g., 7GB), pairing logic for wearables, or detailed regional restrictions beyond U.S. English. Agent B addresses all categories, includes pairing prerequisites (Watch + compatible iPhone), OS minimums (e.g., iOS 18.1, visionOS 2.1, watchOS 11.1), and provides regional/language rollout details including EU delays—closely matching the checklist.

Finally, User Experience & Structure: Both are well formatted, but Agent B is more comprehensive, structured with clear numbered sections, and includes richer constraints (language, region, rollout timeline). Agent A is concise but incomplete and factually incorrect regarding supported categories.

Because Agent A fails the core entity requirement (DEEP failure) and misses major checklist elements, while Agent B covers all required categories with strong structure and detail, Agent B is MUCH BETTER.
